op_value: Concretely for ops: less manual status chasing, less reconciliation overhead, and a named owner for incidents. That is the before/after change, not a generic platform claim.

scope: Scope boundary in one line: Mellow owns contractor docs, KYC, payment-chain operations, and audit trail; your legal/finance team owns legal qualification and strategic risk decisions. No overclaiming beyond that line.

Concretely for ops: less manual status chasing, less reconciliation overhead, and a named owner for incidents. That is the before/after change, not a generic platform claim.
incident: Objection answer with mechanism: define incident trigger, named owner, response SLA, and the exact artifact trail produced after each escalation.

price: Acknowledge the fee gap directly, then reframe through all-in economics, audit trail, payment-chain reliability, and reduced finance fire drills.
Do not defend price in the abstract. Name the cheaper anchor, then explain what risk and manual work still remain outside that cheaper headline rate.

category: Reset the map simply: CoR, EoR, CM, and direct contracts solve different operating problems. Pick the right contour before pitching benefits.
Plain-language clarification beats jargon. Diagnose the buyer model first, then explain why the recommended structure fits.

pricing: State fee plus FX mechanics upfront. Trust rises when the buyer sees the full cost picture before committing.
Treat all-in economics as part of the product conversation, not a small-print finance detail.

risk: Separate what Mellow controls from what external banks or sanctions regimes control. Honest boundaries matter more than reassurance here.
If the situation is structurally unstable, say so and propose a realistic next diagnostic step instead of forcing a close.
